sentences.text: The-- the ideology of the hostel, the Tylers Green hostel for boys was more of an [NON-ENGLISH] ideology, the ideology.
Yeah.
And-- I'm going to come to this.
And then-- and then-- so-- so-- and if we had somebody come down from London and it was like we had a little a [NON-ENGLISH] group there, junior group for children, you know.
So that was that orientation.
And-- and then when I got to London, I must've joined up or got connected with B'nai Akiva.
And what it was, I was working in the furniture factory in London, and I got two weeks' vacation.
And I didn't have that much money, you know.
So what they had was they had camps then.
There was still-- a war was still going on.
And they had these people picking apples, kids, boys and girls picking apples, and adults too, because they couldn't get farm workers.
We were picking apples for the war effort on large apple farms.
So the B'nai Akiva-- I'm sure the [NON-ENGLISH] too-- they had a little-- some tents they pitched in some-- in some field, for the farmer's field.
And they're picking this farmer's apples.
And therefore, the cost of these camps was almost nil, because he had to pay-- he had to pay for the-- getting his apples picked.
And that covered the cost of the-- of the camp.
So I had no expenses, so I was able to go to this camp for two weeks.
And I guess this other kid had been bothered because I went to a B'nai Akiva camp rather than to the [NON-ENGLISH] camp, you know.
But the same God looked upon us both.
And we kept the same Shabbos, had the same prayers, ate the same kind of kosher food.
And the girls camps were maybe in some field 5 or 10 blocks away, 10-- 5 or 10 fields away.
They kept boys and girls different, teenagers, which was probably not a bad idea.
And so-- particularly for the parents of religious girls, you know.
So-- so that was that.

And my activities when I-- when I came to-- to London, in High Wycombe already, while I was there in the year, I went to apprentice classes to the High Wycombe school, in High Wycombe, to apprentice classes for cabinetmaking.
So I used-- after work was down the street, a few blocks down the street, I'd go from the factory to apprentice classes for a couple of hours.
And then also-- a couple of times a week.
And then also couple times a week I went to a youth club, still in High Wycombe.
I got into that habit.
And we did boxing.
We played ping-pong.
Got into boxing there, and ping-pong.
On Sundays, we took bicycle rides to the country.
And I was the only Jewish-- there was another Jewish boy there.
And they used to call me Jew boy, but it was friendly.
It was not-- it was not-- you know.
And-- and there was another Jewish boy.
But did not refer to him as Jew boy.
They did not refer to him as such.
I guess he didn't-- whatever it was.
And-- but it was friendly and it was pleasant.
So when I got into London, we had started a certain system of doing things.
So I went to classes, took classes in the evening, classes.
What sort of classes?
For-- I think it was in relation to-- to either-- either cabinet-- it was not cabinet, specifically.
I was , I mean, maybe arithmetic or English, sort of in relation to that-- in relation to that.
And I also joined a boys club in London, the Jewish boys' clubs in London, where I also did boxing and played ping-pong, [LAUGHS] and whatever they do there, you know.
And so that-- what I was into.
And there was the other boy what I was living with was not into those things.
I also joined B'nai Akiva.
And B'nai Akiva had meetings every Shabbos, which means every Sabbath.
We had meetings.
And-- and so that kept my-- my-- I had a pretty full plate, going-- having a full-time work.
And in those days, a job was maybe-- even when I was 14 years old, was more than 40 hours.
I don't know how many more than 40 hours.
You know, it wasn't just a 40 hour, like it now in America, you know?
There were more hours.
And the kid then, a 14-year-old kid, wasn't a child.
You know, a 14-year-old kid was already somebody who was either learning a trade or making a living.
It was a different atmosphere, a different evaluation.
So in a sense, I never got to be a teenager where you do nothing but go to school and drive a car and whatever-- they have promiscuous sex.
I never got into these things, these fun things.
So it was pretty well more of a-- and I had a lot of ideology, was quite motivated by ideology, especially from-- I got it from the Akiva towards Israel.
There was a B'nai Akiva, so there was a part of Mizrachi.
And the ideology was always war time.
And nobody could go to Israel.
The ideology was to go to Israel or be involved with Israel.
And we sang Jewish songs and we got lectures about Judaism and all that.
And it's quite innocent.
It was quite an innocent situation.
It was not boy meet girl, though there were boys and girls together.
And-- and so from there, these were my-- essentially my activities.
And-- and I also read a newspaper.
I began to read the newspapers.
I bought different newspapers, different newspapers.
I realized different-- sort of different opinions.
But at 14 years old, already did that.
And I used to read a newspaper from page one to the last page, like, you know nothing, no selectivity, but just get ideas of that newspaper.
And that kept me while I was going to [INAUDIBLE].
So all these, between them, there really wasn't much time left except to eat and sleep after all these activities.
And so-- and then-- then, at age 16, I didn't-- I sort of didn't like the factory anymore.
And I wanted-- somebody told me about another area called diamond mounting.
And I think I had a stye in my eye.
Some dust came into my head, and like, you know, had problems with my eye.
Because of that, because of dust getting in my-- so I wanted to leave that factory, and I did.
And-- and I got a job in-- in what they call Manhattan Garden, the diamond area, district area in London.
That was in-- must have been in 1945, in-- middle of '45 somewhere.
And I got a job there.
And I've worked in that for a year, 'til '47.
And then I wanted to go to hakhshara.
Because of my involvement with B'nai Akiva and the ideology towards hakhshara.
I wanted to go-- hakhshara means preparation.
That's centers of preparation to prepare kids to live what they called-- then it was Palestine.
There was no Israel then.
It was called Palestine.
And the-- the name given to the-- to Israel by the Romans, Palestina, and used ever since.
And so that was a year prior to the-- '46 was prior to the-- to the state of Israel.
And the boss was a very nice Jewish man.
And I think he took me in as an apprentice there because I was Jewish and he felt sorry for me, wanted to help me to learn the trade.
And when I was going to leave, [INAUDIBLE] says, you're nuts.
You're crazy.
You'll never be able to get into Israel.
The British had the mandate.
They were keeping Jews out.
They were making sure to keep Jews out.
And there was a very small quota system, maybe it was in the 5 or 10, whatever it was a year for each country.
And you'll never get there.
You going to parachute in?
And he tried to dissuade me, because he meant well on my behalf.
And I also had a cousin who says, don't do it.
You're nuts.
Learn a trade.
You have a chance to learn a good skill now, make a nice living.
Anyway, I did go to hakhshara in 1946.
The war ended in 1945 and I was in London.

I was in London, working in the-- either just finishing off with the woodwork factory-- I was just finishing off with the woodwork factory, because while the-- because while the war was on, the woodwork factory wouldn't release me and I had to hang on.
I had to stay on.
But as soon as the war ended, which I think was in May, '45, I was able to leave and start the job in the-- with the-- go to the other job.
That's where I was, in London.
And at that time, they were-- they were sending off these rockets, these rockets called [NON-ENGLISH].
They were these-- these rockets from V-1 and V-2s, from the-- they were firing them from Germany, from Europe, into England.
And-- and they did a lot-- a great deal of damage, a great deal of damage.
And on Friday afternoons, I used to go to a bathhouse.
It was a place where you take baths, maybe-- and it had different stores, maybe hundreds of bath tubs in it with curtains around it.
You paid maybe sixpence, maybe not, maybe a quarter, and you took a bath.
And I got a good bath there, because I was staying in-- in the-- in the boarding house, the boarding-- the apartment.
There was also a bath tub, but you had to keep on putting money into it all the time, the gas.
And it wasn't so good.
This was better, bigger bath.
I could thrash around in it, you know, and take my time in it.
And it was always hot water.
So I did it every Friday.
And one Friday, we are getting ready for Shabbos, and so that-- only one bath a week.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10 minutes after I left-- for some reason, I used to hang around there and thrash in the water there and splash around and just sort of hang out, stay on longer there.
But for some reason, this Friday I want-- I somehow, for no particular reason, want to get out of there, you know.

I didn't-- so 10 minutes after I left, one of these rockets hit a direct hit on the bathhouse and everybody, maybe a couple hundred people, killed instantaneously, the staff and all these people taking these-- [INAUDIBLE] there were several hundred bathtubs on many floors, you know-- were killed immediately.
And so that was-- was 10 minutes before.
For some reason-- I don't know why I left.
I would have stayed longer normally, maybe another half an hour, hung around and splashed around and you know.
And I was only a kid.
I mean, I was only 15 years old, you know.
So-- And these are my memories of-- but I had good memories of London.
I had a good time-- I-- in the boys club and going to school at night and stimulation.
And I worked and worked with good people.
And-- and in those days, a lot of Jews were-- were maybe even Communist-oriented working people.
In those days, back in the Second World War, before people came out of Russia, people thought not that Israel was a country, but that-- that the paradise was-- the Messianic Age was in Russia.
That was all ideology.
And the-- at lunchtime, the workmen got together.
They were talking.
And I joined the union.
When I was 15 years old, I joined the union.
And they had a special political meeting.
I joined that.
And-- and there were people trying to influence me to join the Junior Communist Party.
And they had the-- they-- they claimed they had a lot of fun there, a lot of girls there, and a lot of parties, it was fun, to induce people to join the youth movement of the Communist Party.
And of course, being in B'nai Akiva, having that ideology, and knowing that-- that the Communists were against godliness, did not believe in God, I didn't join them, fortunately.
But there were certainly enough inducements.
And I also began to read.
I used to-- my first thing whenever I went to a new neighborhood was to get a library card.
But I also was beginning to buy books.
In where I worked in this diamond thing then, in Manhattan Island, they were selling books, second-hand books off barrels-- off barrels-- you know that barrels?
And they were selling secondhand books for sixpence.
And I was buying these secondhand books on political science and sociology and different political books and reading them.
And-- and so I got a little bit of education out of it-- a little bit of informal education out of it, and-- because my formal education only went to 8th grade in a village school, public school.
So I got a little bit education out of it.
And I also became-- began to hunger for education.
And-- and then subsequently, when I went on hakhshara, we-- that was in Buckinghamshire, in-- in a town called Buckingham.
We had-- and again, it was possibly because we lived in-- in a couple of housing-- housing there-- housing units there-- huts there.
And they were used by the Minister of Agriculture to house women agricultural workers.
But then they put us into them, men and women.
And we used to go out to work for different farmers.
You know, we-- there was-- hakhshara didn't have any agriculture themselves.
But we were-- we went out to work for different farmers, one or two people from each different farmer.
I would say in that particular one, there were many hakhsharat in England.
These was from Chabad, also from the Mizrachi, [NON-ENGLISH], which is a religious-- the-- the organization senior to B'nai Akiva.
B'nai Akiva graduated [INAUDIBLE] from 18 into [NON-ENGLISH], which-- and the organization of religious Jews.
And this particular hakhshara, I'm going to say about maybe 40, 50 people, maybe half women, half men.
And the men were-- lived separately and the women lived separately.
Yeah.
To-- for prepare for-- hakhshara means preparation, preparations for Israel.
In those days, you thought of Israel in terms of agriculture.
There wasn't much else there.
And so-- and then, of course, one or two people got these affidavits to go to Israel, just one or two.
And then '48-- then in '48, of course, things changed and Israel became a Jewish state.
And we went on Aliyah.
That means going up to Israel.
When you go to Israel, you say-- Aliyah means going up, because Israel is regarded as the central focal point.
And you go off to Israel.
And so we went on Aliyah illegally.
There was still then the illegal Aliyah.
The British still after May still were controlling the ports, you know.
And they didn't-- and so on.
And so we-- and they certainly wouldn't have let people out of England to go to Israel, because they were against it at the time.
So we went officially to-- to Switzerland, on vacation to Switzerland, on a few weeks' vacation, so we had all these working clothes and things and boots and things.
And then when we were in Marseilles-- when we were in Dover-- in Le Havre, we went down by train to Marseilles in preparation of going on one of those illegal boats, Aliyah Bet boats to Israel.
And when I got to Israel-- Do you remember the name of the boat?
I can't think of it.
I do-- I-- it's one of the big names, because it had-- these were cargo ships.
These were cargo ships.
And they put in lots and lots of layers of bunks, you know, maybe four or five layers of bunks and full-- where the cargo holds were.
And people slept on those bunks.
And some did go out on deck a little bit.
And-- and it wasn't very comfortable, but it got people there.
And they had bought several of these cargo ships and refitted them.
And they had some Jews-- some [NON-ENGLISH] Jews, [NON-ENGLISH], who had served in the American navy or British navy during the war who knew how to run ships.
I mean, the ship, you have to know how to do it.
There has to be a captain and the other people, engineers and whatnot, you know, how to run a boat, run a ship.
So they were-- they had that knowledge and experience and they were doing that.
And so when I got to Israel, I joined the Israeli Air Force.
This was an illegal [INTERPOSING VOICES].
It was still illegal.
We got in.
They-- I think the British had already left then, because the British, I believe, left in May, in May '48.
And then-- but still they had some kind of control.
But anyway, we got in.
We got in.
Somehow we got in.
I don't know exactly what the-- what the-- we got in.
There's no problem.
We got off the boat and we were there, you know.
There was no-- I don't think the-- the British stopped the exit from England to Israel.
But once in Israel, we got there, we were able to get in.
It was very-- the British, if I remember, had pulled out in May-- 19 in May '48, hoping that the Arabs would then move in and take over Israel, [DRINKS], which of course they tried to do.

Seven different-- several different Arab nations who had regular armies, including Trans-Jordan Army, trained by the British, began to move in or tried to move in and by all normal circumstances should have taken Israel, where they never had any-- really an army as such.
They had the underground organizations of [NON-ENGLISH] and-- and the-- and others and--
Palmach.
Palmach.
And the one from [INAUDIBLE], what was that?
Etzel.
Huh?
Etzel and Haganah.
What?
Etzel.
Etzel and Haganah.
There were all these-- they went.
But those were really small game stuff.
They were really mostly people with rifles.
They're just mostly people with rifles and rifles-- and there weren't tanks and aeroplanes involved in that.
And maybe they had a few trucks.
There weren't any tanks involved in that, so-- while the British were there.
And-- and they had rifles maybe-- Napoleon, from Napoleon's time still, rifles.
They got rifles wherever they could, and ammunition.
So it was kind of a rag tail army.
And by all calculations, the Arabs should have just walked in and taking over.
No problem.
That's what the Egyptian-- what the British estimated from the intelligence estimated.
They had a pretty good estimate.
But they forgot one factor.
They forgot that there is a God and-- that there is a God and that God had destined for the Jewish people to get the land of Israel, [NON-ENGLISH].
And by all calculation, they should never have gotten it.
There was no way possibly.
They had nothing.
And they had a few Piper Cub-- Piper Cub planes.
These are two-seater planes, little light-wing two-seater planes people use for sport or for-- you know, for sport, for just for flying around for hobbies.
They had a few of those.
And-- and-- and at one point they were taking trucks or-- or tractors and putting around them to make it look-- wood around them, you know, fit them with wood around, make it look like a tank.
Of course, a tractor had these tractor wheels like a trank-- like a tank.
So when they moved forward, the Arabs thought a lot of tanks coming in.
And they had these things, these same tractors going up on ships, up and down, all day and all night.

So the Arabs are thinking there are hundreds of tanks coming in, you know, when it was just these regular tractors used for farm work with a wooden facade around them built to make it look like a tank.
And they had nothing.
So the Arabs should have taken it.
They were pretty close in getting it.
They were right close to Tel Aviv.
The Egyptian air force was bombing Tel Aviv.
And-- but by the grace of God-- after the [NON-ENGLISH], the grace of God, it didn't happen.
What was your involvement when you arrived in Israel?
My involvement, I first of all was-- went to my kibbutz.
We had a kibbutz.
And we went to hakhshara in-- with that kibbutz, those people.
To [NON-ENGLISH].
To-- no.
To [NON-ENGLISH] and a couple of other places, we distributed.
[NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and what's one near [NON-ENGLISH], one-- one near [NON-ENGLISH]?
Yes.
Something-- one-- and maybe [NON-ENGLISH], right near it.
And I was-- I was in that.
I think it was [NON-ENGLISH].
And we're different-- you know, the kibbutz are going to take so many.
We're there on-- for the what they call a [NON-ENGLISH], forming a [NON-ENGLISH], forming a formation, the essence of a [NON-ENGLISH] today.
And then they established I think in '49 Kibbutz Lavi.
Kibbutz Lavi now have 600 people on them.
And they are near Tiberias.
It's a big kibbutz.
They also have several-- several-- they have a hotel of several big apartment building which are like hotels and a big dining hall.
That's one of their businesses.
And they have different people there.
They have-- they have about 600 people there all together now.
But the essence was the people coming from-- of course, other Israelis joined them and others joined them eventually.
But they had-- the essence came from England, from the various hakhshara in different parts of England, and then landed up in Kibbutz Lavi.
Yeah.
So-- but then, after a couple of weeks I-- I was there.
And I wanted to join up with the Air Force.
I had a dream of being a pilot.
And I want to join the Air Force.
So I went down.
And [NON-ENGLISH] they had then-- they had then the [NON-ENGLISH], the pilot's called it [NON-ENGLISH].
It wasn't a pilot-- it wasn't [INAUDIBLE] flying done, just a couple of houses, which was called the pilot school, where the people were lined up in preparation to being sent out of the country.
There was no training in Israel for pilots at that point.
They were being sent to Czechoslovakia to be trained there, so they were making selections of people.
So I was in that thing there, what they called [NON-ENGLISH], although no pilot training was taking place.
Mostly we-- most of the day was spent playing volleyball.
[LAUGHS] You know, so were just hanging around, you know.
And then some did get sent to Czechoslovakia.
And some were for different reasons ruled out.
I never got to be a pilot.
I was ruled out of there for some reason, fortunately for myself, because I subsequently met another fellow there about a year later on the beach in Tel Aviv.
And he told me that most of those guys were dead already.

Most of the guys on that [NON-ENGLISH] were dead, because that was-- at that-- the kind of planes they had and I don't think they would be allowed to fly under that-- the FF-- the-- America would not allow those planes to fly in America.
You couldn't land at an airport with a lot of those planes.
And a lot of them were dead, or seriously injured, if not dead.
So I was sorry at the time.
Then it was the biggest blow to me.
But-- as a kid 19 years old, that's what I wanted to do.
But-- but looking back, it was a lucky break.
I got a lucky break and I'm still alive today.
[LAUGHS] So then-- then I was in the army.
I was transferred to the army.
No.
And then I was in the air force.
I was at an-- a couple of airbases.
And I was working out of the supply room, putting supply room.
There were supplies to be issued.
And-- and Ezer Weizman was an officer there.
He was only a lieutenant there.
He had been-- towards the end of the war, he had joined the-- the British Air Force and was a Sergeant.
And he flew a-- a Spitfire.
He learned to-- so he was only like a-- a pilot.

So when the other pilots for [NON-ENGLISH], the American and British pilots and other South African pilots, who had actually fought-- had fighting experience during the war, used to keep an eye on him, you know that sort of-- a new pilot comes in, the other pilots keep an eye on you, because they don't let you get caught in the middle somewhere by yourself.
And they kept an eye on him.
But one day he did come back and he did have a-- he scored.
And he-- he downed a Russian plane.
What was happening, there were several planes going down to the Eilat area somewhere.
And they were just flying around, but four planes or so.
And I was from-- I can't think now the airport there.
It was in the south.
It was near [NON-ENGLISH] and they used to fly down there.
And then four Russian MiGs used to come up, fly above them, chase them away.
And they would run, of course, with those-- those little flames, I saw the Russian MiGs.
Of course they took off.
And the Russians were having a little bit of fun.
And then one day, the-- the Yiddish [INAUDIBLE], you know, the-- the Jewish brains, they let those four little guys fly there again in those little planes there.
And then MiGs came out after them.
But they also had another-- another group of people flying above, whom the Russians weren't aware of, didn't see.
So when the Russians came out to chase these guys, they swooped down on them and knocked off four Russian MiGs with the-- with the inferior planes.
And Ezer Weizmann got one-- scored one of them.
People think he got to be president of a state because his uncle was president.
No.
He earned it the hard way.
He was an officer and a good officer.
Then he became commander after the other ones, the-- there was a fellow who was from South Africa who was a commander of that-- that particular [NON-ENGLISH] 101-- flight 101-- Squadron 101.
And I can't think of the name of the place.
I can't think-- it was in [INAUDIBLE] there.
There was an airport in [INAUDIBLE], another one near [INAUDIBLE].
And all the British had left these airports.
The British had built all these airports and army places for their army all over the place, you know?
So this is what the Israeli-- the-- this is where the-- basically Haganah moved in.
The Haganah moved into the airports where the British left behind.
And army units moved in where the British had built barracks and other things.
And so he got one.
And then he subsequently became commander of this squadron subsequently.
And then he worked his way up and became chief of the Air Force.

And I think he had a big hand, if not the primary hand, when they had this-- when they destroyed the-- the-- the Egyptian Air Force and other air forces, when they did this-- when they-- by surprise, caught him by surprise.
And they were flying there and destroyed most of the bridge of the Egyptian Air Force and other air forces on the ground.
I was-- I was there for about-- in the air force.
I was in the air force for-- [INAUDIBLE] it must've been for about '48, '49.
[INAUDIBLE] '50, maybe a year, year and a half altogether.
Then at that point, people want to get out, get discharged, move ahead with their lives, learn a trade or something.
And I also saw people-- a lot of people leaving, because after '49, the war was won.
The war, the '48 war was won in '49.
Leaving-- some left the country.
Some Israelis left to go back to home, get the jobs and get married, you know.
So people were leaving, both from the [NON-ENGLISH], volunteers from other countries, Britain, France.
They had families wherever they came from.
They went home.
They did their job and they went home.
OK.
We're just going to switch tapes.
Yeah.
